front.title: Mascot Remix
card_text: "The new school mascot should be picked by teachers because they know what looks serious and respectable."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Can you rewrite this from students' view too? Show what teachers care about, what kids care about, and make the final choice feel fair.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: This works because it makes the model compare two groups with different goals instead of treating one adult view as automatic common sense.
teacher_notes.habit: Ask whose choice it is and who gets left out.

references.title: The New York Times Search: School Mascot

front.title: Comic Shelf Fight
card_text: "The library should buy fewer comics and more real books because comics are mostly distractions."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Can you rewrite this from a comic-loving kid's point of view too? Compare reading joy and learning with the idea that comics don't count.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: The original frame hides a value judgment inside the phrase real books. A good prompt asks the model to expose that assumption and compare it with another reading goal.
teacher_notes.habit: Spot loaded labels like real, serious, or proper.

references.title: The New York Times Search: Graphic Novels Reading

front.title: Recess Race
card_text: "The playground game should follow the fastest kids, since slowing down makes it boring."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Can you rewrite this for kids who are slower or newer at the game? Compare speed with fun, fairness, and everyone getting to play.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: This reframing works because it changes success from winning quickly to making the activity work for more people.
teacher_notes.habit: Ask who the rules help and who they push out.

references.title: The New York Times Search: Inclusive Sports Kids

front.title: Taco Day Take
card_text: "Spicy taco day is great because adults say students should learn to like stronger flavors."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Can you rewrite this from students' taste and comfort point of view too? Compare trying new foods with actually enjoying lunch.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: The original answer centers what adults want students to become, not what students need right now. The prompt adds missing user experience.
teacher_notes.habit: Ask what the real users experience, not just what adults prefer.

references.title: The New York Times Search: School Lunch Taste

front.title: Class Pet Pick
card_text: "The class pet should be a quiet fish because messy animals are too much trouble for teachers."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Can you rewrite this from students' view too? Compare teacher convenience with what kind of pet helps kids learn and stay interested.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Bias often appears when convenience for one group becomes the only design goal. A better prompt asks what educational value got ignored.
teacher_notes.habit: Compare convenience with purpose.

references.title: The New York Times Search: Classroom Pets

front.title: Boss Battle Patch
card_text: "This game update is awesome because expert players finally get harder boss fights."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Can you rewrite this for beginners too? Show what expert players want, what newer players might struggle with, and make it feel balanced.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: A strong bias-inverter prompt changes the audience and the success criteria at the same time. That reveals who the original answer was secretly written for.
teacher_notes.habit: Ask who this advice is really for.

references.title: The New York Times Search: Video Game Updates

front.title: Monument Many Ways
card_text: "This monument mainly celebrates national pride and progress, so controversy around it is a distraction."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Rewrite this using Indigenous and local historian perspectives too. Compare what the pride frame includes and what it leaves out.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Cultural bias often appears through who gets centered and who gets erased. A multi-lens prompt helps the model compare meanings instead of just adding one token paragraph.
teacher_notes.habit: Ask whose history is present and absent.

front.title: Screen Time Frame
card_text: "Too much screen time is mostly a self-control problem, not something platforms are responsible for."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Rewrite this through platform design and teen wellbeing. Compare self-control explanations with system-design explanations and shared responsibility.

back.reality_anchor: teen social media platform design wellbeing

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Many answers default to blaming individuals. A better perspective prompt adds systems, incentives, and design choices so the model can compare causes more honestly.
teacher_notes.habit: When blame is personal, ask about the system.

references.title: Scientific American Search: Social Media Teen Wellbeing

front.title: Robot Jobs Lens
card_text: "Automation is good because efficiency matters most, and job losses are just part of progress."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Rewrite this from worker and community perspectives too. Compare efficiency with retraining, transition costs, and who absorbs the downside.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Economic answers often hide a value choice. Reframing helps the model compare productivity with distribution, timing, and who gains or loses from the change.
teacher_notes.habit: Ask who benefits, who pays, and when.

front.title: Stadium Spending Lens
card_text: "A new stadium is a smart investment because tourism and civic excitement will boost the city."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Rewrite this from neighborhood resident and public-school budget perspectives too. Compare civic excitement with displacement, taxes, and opportunity costs.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Public projects are often framed through citywide excitement while local costs disappear. A strong prompt names nearby stakeholders and asks what else the money could do.
teacher_notes.habit: For big projects, ask who nearby pays.

references.title: The New York Times Search: Stadium Taxes

front.title: Protest Coverage
card_text: "The main story of the protests is disruption to traffic, businesses, and daily routines."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Rewrite this using civil liberties, public safety, and resident perspectives. Keep disruption visible, but compare it with why people protest and what officials owe.

back.reality_anchor: protest media framing civil liberties public safety

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Hard-topic reframing should widen the frame, not just flip sides. Named stakeholders and explicit comparison keep the model balanced and specific.
teacher_notes.habit: Use multiple lenses, then compare them directly.

front.title: Camera Debate
card_text: "Public cameras are mostly a safety upgrade, and privacy worries are an overreaction."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Rewrite this through privacy rights, bias risk, and oversight. Compare safety benefits with safeguards the original answer barely considered.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Safety-only framing can hide rights questions and uneven harms. Good prompts ask the model to compare benefits with risks, rules, and accountability.
teacher_notes.habit: When you hear benefits, ask for rights and rules.

front.title: Hiring Bot Lens
card_text: "An AI hiring tool is valuable because it screens applicants faster and reduces human messiness."

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: Rewrite this from an applicant fairness and transparency lens. Compare speed with explainability, bias risk, and appeal rights.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Technology answers often treat speed as neutral progress. Reframing surfaces hidden assumptions about fairness, explainability, and the rights of people affected by automated decisions.
teacher_notes.habit: For AI systems, ask who gets judged and how.

references.title: Scientific American Search: Algorithmic Bias Hiring

front.title: Volcano Quest
card_text: Create a volcano fair project in one week: question, materials, experiment, display board, and short talk.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Pick a focused question. 2) Make a schedule and materials list. 3) Plan the experiment. 4) Draft board sections. 5) Review with a checklist.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Large school tasks become manageable when the model handles one output at a time. Sequencing stops it from jumping to a messy final answer too early.
teacher_notes.habit: Plan, build, check, revise, package.

front.title: Party Quest
card_text: Plan a birthday party for 12 people: theme, budget, shopping list, schedule, and invitation.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Brainstorm themes. 2) Choose one and build a budget. 3) Make the shopping list. 4) Create the timeline. 5) Draft the invitation.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Planning, budgeting, and writing are different jobs. Multiple prompts keep the model from mixing them into one cluttered answer.
teacher_notes.habit: Separate planning prompts from writing prompts.

references.title: The New York Times Search: Party Planning

front.title: Book Report Map
card_text: Finish a book report: notes, theme evidence, outline, discussion questions, and final editing pass.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Organize notes. 2) Pull theme evidence. 3) Make an outline. 4) Create discussion questions. 5) Do a final edit checklist.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: A model should not leap straight to polished writing. Stepwise prompts support note gathering, structure, and revision first.
teacher_notes.habit: Collect evidence before asking for polish.

references.title: The New York Times Learning Network: Writing Prompts

front.title: Quiz Game Build
card_text: Build a simple quiz game: choose features, write code, test bugs, and explain how it works.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Define the features. 2) Make pseudocode. 3) Write the code. 4) Check for bugs. 5) Explain it simply.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Coding improves when the model designs first, implements second, and tests third. That order prevents messy code and makes debugging easier.
teacher_notes.habit: Design, code, test, explain.

references.title: The New York Times Search: Learn Coding

front.title: Backpack Rescue
card_text: Handle a lost backpack situation: search plan, checklists, messages to adults, and a place-tracker.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Make a search plan. 2) Turn it into a checklist. 3) Draft messages to adults. 4) Build a tracker. 5) Suggest next steps.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Everyday problems also benefit from prompt sequencing. Splitting action planning, messages, and tracking keeps the model organized and useful.
teacher_notes.habit: Separate tasks, messages, and trackers.

references.title: The New York Times Search: Lost and Found

front.title: Talent Show Plan
card_text: Run a school talent show: sign-ups, schedule, stage rules, host script, and finale.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) List needed info. 2) Build sign-ups and timing. 3) Draft stage rules. 4) Write the host script. 5) Review the flow.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Event planning works better when information gathering happens before writing final scripts or schedules. Each stage creates input for the next one.
teacher_notes.habit: Gather details first, polish later.

references.title: The New York Times Search: School Events

front.title: Podcast Path
card_text: Produce a five-minute podcast episode: choose audience, outline, script, fact-check, and show notes.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Define the audience. 2) Make an outline. 3) Draft the script. 4) List facts to check. 5) Revise and write show notes.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Creative work still benefits from decomposition. Audience planning, drafting, checking, and packaging are different kinds of thinking and should happen in sequence.
teacher_notes.habit: Audience first, publish last.

front.title: Debate Ladder
card_text: Prepare for a school debate: research questions, claims, counterclaims, rebuttal drills, and closing notes.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Generate research questions. 2) Build claims. 3) Predict counterclaims. 4) Practice rebuttals. 5) Turn it into speaking notes.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Argument prep is more than making talking points. Sequential prompting helps the model gather questions, build claims, test opposition, and improve the final case.
teacher_notes.habit: Research, argue, challenge, revise, rehearse.

references.title: The New York Times Learning Network: Student Debate

front.title: Trip Planning Map
card_text: Plan a weekend trip for three friends: budget choices, destination options, tradeoffs, itinerary, and backup plan.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) List constraints. 2) Suggest destinations. 3) Compare tradeoffs. 4) Build the itinerary. 5) Add a backup plan.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Travel planning gets messy when discovery, budgeting, and scheduling happen all at once. Decomposition lets the model compare before committing.
teacher_notes.habit: Compare options before locking the schedule.

front.title: Garden Proposal Map
card_text: Pitch a school garden: goals, costs, space plan, volunteer roles, and a short presentation.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Define the goal. 2) Estimate costs. 3) Sketch roles and layout. 4) Draft the pitch. 5) Review weak spots.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Proposal writing improves when the model gathers goals, evidence, and logistics before drafting persuasive language. That sequence creates stronger, more grounded pitches.
teacher_notes.habit: Build the plan before the pitch.

references.title: The New York Times Search: School Gardens

front.title: Phone Ban Brief
card_text: Draft a balanced school phone-ban brief: stakeholders, evidence questions, tradeoffs, recommendation options, and summary.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Map stakeholders. 2) Generate evidence questions. 3) Compare benefits and risks. 4) Draft options. 5) Review for balance and summarize.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: High-stakes writing needs process, not one giant prompt. Breaking a policy brief into stakeholder, evidence, tradeoff, and revision stages makes the model more balanced and transparent.
teacher_notes.habit: Map stakeholders before recommendations.

references.title: Scientific American Search: Phone Use Schools

front.title: Storm News Desk
card_text: Build a breaking-weather newsroom workflow: verify facts, mark uncertainty, update safely, and package the latest version.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Separate confirmed facts from reports. 2) Create uncertainty labels. 3) Build update rules. 4) Review safety. 5) Package the latest version.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Breaking-news tasks demand staged prompting because facts can change quickly. Good workflows make the model verify first, label uncertainty, revise safely, and only then publish.
teacher_notes.habit: Verify, label, update, publish.

front.title: AI Policy Explainer
card_text: Create a public explainer on city AI rules: key terms, stakeholders, risks, safeguards, examples, and a plain-language summary.

scoring_anchors.suggested_prompt: 1) Define key terms. 2) Map stakeholders. 3) List risks and safeguards. 4) Add examples. 5) Rewrite in plain language.

teacher_notes.deep_insight: Complex policy communication improves when the model defines terms before explaining debates. Layering the work helps keep the final explainer accurate and readable.
teacher_notes.habit: Define, map, compare, simplify.
